lesson.menu_desc: Block 3 review: structure a full conference talk, describe data, explain methods and cause-effect, and deliver under the spotlight -- 30 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 15 -- Review: Conference Presentation
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Patricia Ruffo | Aula 15 -- Review: Conference Presentation

listenings.text: Good morning, everyone, and welcome to the Abbott research symposium. Thank you for joining this plenary session. Let me give you a quick overview of my talk. Firstly, I will explain the framework and the methods we used. Moving on, I will describe the main trends in the data. Then I will discuss what causes these results, and finally I will summarize the key takeaway. So, let me begin with the methods. The data was collected over six months, and the samples were analyzed in our central laboratory using a standard protocol. Now, moving on to the results. If you look at the chart, the main marker showed a steady increase in the first quarter, reached a clear peak, and then settled into a plateau. The proportion of patients who responded well rose from about forty percent to nearly seventy percent. I would like to emphasize one important point. The improvement is strongly correlated with the new treatment, but correlation is not causation, so we controlled for the main variables before we drew any conclusion. To summarize, the new framework is more accurate, the trend is clearly positive, and the effect is significant. The main takeaway is simple: a clear structure and honest data build trust with your audience. Thank you very much for your attention, and I look forward to your questions.

Hi Patricia, this is David from the communications team. I just listened to the recording of your rehearsal for the symposium, and overall it is excellent. I want to give you some honest critique before the big day. First, the structure is very clear. The signposts are strong, and the audience always knows where you are. That is great. Second, on the methods slide, you speak a little too fast, so please work on your pacing. Slow down, and leave a short pause after each key number. Third, when you describe the trends, try to engage the audience more. Ask a short question, or point at the chart, so they look up from their notes. And one small thing about the cause and effect part. You explain correlation and causation very well, but you deliver it in a flat voice. Add a little energy there, because that is the most important point of the talk. Practice it two or three more times, focus on pacing and delivery, and you will be ready. Good luck, and let me know if you want one more rehearsal.

extra_audio.text: First, open with an overview and present the agenda. Next, explain your framework and your methods. After that, describe the main trends in the data. Then, discuss the cause and effect behind the results. Finally, recap the key points and deliver one clear takeaway.
